name: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh
epithet: The Man for Whom the Throne Shook
title: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (RA): The Man for Whom the Throne Shook
meta_description: The story of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (RA), the chief of the Aws in Madinah whose people accepted Islam in a single day, who stood with the Prophet Muhammad at Badr, and at whose death the Throne of the Most Merciful shook.
keywords: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, story of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, Saad ibn Muadh, chief of the Aws, Ansar, Battle of the Trench, sahaba stories, The Firsts
lede: He was the leader of his tribe, the man everyone in Madinah turned to. He had a kingdom within reach, and people who would have followed him to it. He gave all of that away in a single afternoon, after sitting down to listen to words he had come to silence.

This is the story of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (may Allah be pleased with him): the chief of the Aws, the man whose people entered Islam in one day, and the believer at whose death the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ said the Throne of the Most Merciful shook.

paras: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh was from the Aws, one of the two great tribes of Madinah. His father had been a leader before him and had died in the long wars between the tribes. By his early thirties, Sa'd had become the senior figure his people looked to, the man they trusted to hold a fragile peace together.

He was not old. He was about thirty-one. But when he walked into a gathering, people knew at once that a leader had entered. He had presence. He had weight. He was the kind of man whose word could move a whole tribe in one direction or another.

His family was woven through the city. His mother was of the Khazraj, the other great tribe, and through her he was a cousin of Usayd ibn Hudayr. When he himself accepted Islam, his mother, his wife, his children, and his siblings would all accept it with him.
That is the detail to hold onto. When Islam reached Madinah, it did not first win over the powerless. It reached a man with everything to lose, and asked him to risk it.

sections.h2: He came to stop a teacher
paras: Mus'ab ibn Umayr had been sent to Madinah to teach the new Muslims, and he was doing it in a garden alongside As'ad ibn Zurarah. People kept coming to listen, and people kept leaving changed. Sa'd did not like it. He sent his cousin Usayd ibn Hudayr to put an end to it, and Usayd came back transformed, his face no longer the face he had left with.

So Sa'd went himself, angry, weapon in hand, certain this teacher was a threat to his people. He walked up to the gathering ready to end it. Mus'ab simply offered him a seat. Sit, and listen. If you do not like what you hear, I will leave. Sa'd sat down.

In the space of that one conversation, the anger left him. He put his weapon down. He listened, and then he asked the only question that mattered: how do I enter this? He prayed two units of prayer, and the man who had come to silence Islam carried it back to his tribe.

sections.h2: A whole tribe in a single day
paras: Sa'd did not return quietly. He stood before his people, who gathered around him because he was their chief, and he asked them a question chiefs sometimes ask: how do you regard me among you? They answered as he knew they would. You are the best of us, the most trustworthy, the one we turn to in all our affairs.

Then he said the thing they did not expect. Not one of you, man or woman, will speak to me again until you believe in Allah and His Messenger. It landed like a shock. It was meant to. He had just heard them confess that they depended on him, and now he was telling them that everything between them rested on this.

They asked him what it meant to believe. He told them what he had learned only hours before. And in that single gathering, every person there became Muslim. With the chief of the Aws, the doors of Madinah opened, and a city that had worn itself out in war began to find another way.

sections.h2: The warning he gave a friend
paras: Before Islam, Sa'd had a trade partner in Makkah named Umayyah ibn Khalaf, a man known for his cruelty to the believers. When Sa'd came for pilgrimage, Umayyah hosted him. But as Sa'd made his way around the Kaaba, Abu Jahl confronted him: how dare you walk in safety here when you shelter those who have left us? The two nearly came to blows.

Sa'd turned to his old friend and spoke plainly, not in anger but almost in pity. You are a fool to insist on this path. He told him what he had heard: that the Prophet ﷺ had said Umayyah would be killed. Umayyah went pale, because even his enemies knew the Prophet ﷺ did not lie.

Umayyah went home and told his wife he would never leave Makkah to fight. And he meant it, until pride dragged him out to Badr anyway, where the warning came true. Sa'd had tried to wake a friend. The tragedy was that the friend heard him, and still would not turn.

sections.h2: The truthful one of the Ansar
paras: At Badr, the Muslims were caught unprepared against a far larger army. The Prophet ﷺ gathered the companions and asked their counsel. The men of Makkah spoke first. Then he asked again, and the Ansar understood he was waiting for them, because their pledge had only promised to defend him inside Madinah. Badr was beyond it. They could have turned back with no blame at all.

Sa'd stood, now the senior voice of the Ansar, and he did not turn back. He told the Prophet ﷺ that they had believed in him and followed him, and then he went further. By the One who sent you with the truth, if you marched us into the sea, we would march in with you, and not one of us would stay behind.

For that stand, he is remembered as Siddiq al-Ansar, the truthful one of the Ansar. More than two hundred of the three hundred and thirteen at Badr were his people, and his words carried them. The Prophet's face opened with joy. This was loyalty offered when refusal would have been easy.

At Uhud he stayed close to the Prophet ﷺ when others were scattered, and that day he buried his own brother and his stepson among the fallen. When the city wept for its dead and the Prophet ﷺ grieved for his uncle Hamza, it was Sa'd who gathered the Ansar to come and weep beside him. Your loss is our loss, that gesture said. We will not let you mourn alone.

The hardest test came at the Battle of the Trench, when the tribes massed to destroy Madinah from outside while an ally turned traitor within. The danger pressed in from every side at once. The Qur'an would later describe what those days felt like:

Sa'd was struck in the arm by an arrow during the siege, in the one spot his armour did not cover. The wound would not stop bleeding. As the others rushed to save him, his prayer was not for himself. If there is any fight left with the people who hurt Your Prophet and drove him out, let me live to see it. And if the war is over, then let this be my martyrdom.

He added one more thing, out of love and not revenge: do not let me die until my eyes have seen the matter of the tribe that betrayed us settled. When that tribe later surrendered, they asked for Sa'd as their judge, trusting their old bond with him. He was carried in, near death, and the Prophet ﷺ said to the people, stand for your leader. Sa'd judged with justice, and the Prophet ﷺ said his judgment matched the judgment of Allah.

ayah.text: They massed against you from above and below; your eyes rolled [with fear], your hearts rose into your throats, and you thought [ill] thoughts of God.

paras: Within days, the wound took him. He had been a Muslim for only about six years. The Prophet ﷺ had visited his tent in the mosque every single day, sitting with him for a long time, going to him first after his prayer.

When Sa'd died, the Prophet ﷺ said that the Throne of the Most Merciful shook at the return of his soul. They carried his body and felt almost no weight in it, and the Prophet ﷺ said it was because the angels themselves were carrying him.

Once, the companions admired a fine garment the Prophet ﷺ had been given, the most beautiful cloth they had ever seen on him. He told them not to be dazzled by it. A small handkerchief of Sa'd's in Paradise, he said, is better than this. Sa'd had let go of a throne on earth, and in exchange he was given something no throne could buy.

It is worth remembering what he turned down. When the Prophet ﷺ arrived in Madinah, there were men who saw their own claims to leadership slipping away and resented it. Sa'd had as much to lose as any of them. He could have fought to keep his place at the top of his people. Instead he handed it over for the sake of Allah, and became the man for whom the Throne shook.

lessons_intro: His was a short journey, about six years, and yet it carries a weight that longer lives rarely reach. The reasons are simple, and they are within reach for anyone.

lessons.bold: Be willing to sit and listen.
lessons.text: Sa'd came to silence the truth and left as its servant, because he agreed to sit down and hear it out before deciding. A few honest minutes changed the rest of his life.

lessons.bold: Use whatever standing you have for good.
lessons.text: He did not hide his faith to protect his position. He spent his influence the moment he had something worth spending it on, and a whole tribe followed.

lessons.bold: Loyalty counts most when leaving is allowed.
lessons.text: At Badr he could have walked away without blame. He stayed, and pledged to follow into the sea. The truest commitment is the one given when no one is forcing it.

lessons.bold: Seek what lasts, not what dazzles.
lessons.text: He let go of a kingdom and was promised that even a handkerchief of his in Paradise was worth more. What we chase quietly shapes what we become.

faq.a: He was the chief of the Aws, one of the two main tribes of Madinah, and a close companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. After he accepted Islam, his entire tribe followed in a single day, and he became a leading figure among the Ansar.

faq.q: Why is it said that the Throne shook at his death?
faq.a: When Sa'd died of the wound he received at the Battle of the Trench, the Prophet ﷺ said that the Throne of the Most Merciful shook at the return of his soul. It is understood as a sign of the honour and joy with which his soul was received.

faq.q: Why is Sa'd called the truthful one of the Ansar?
faq.a: At Badr, when the Ansar could have declined to fight without blame, Sa'd stood and pledged that they would follow the Prophet ﷺ even into the sea. That stand of loyalty earned him the title Siddiq al-Ansar, the truthful one of the Ansar.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh?
faq.a: To listen before judging, to use whatever influence we have for good, to stay loyal when leaving would be easy, and to seek what lasts over what merely impresses.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never lead a tribe or stand on a battlefield. But all of us hold something we are afraid to risk: a reputation, a comfort, a place among people who think well of us. Sa'd had more of that than almost anyone, and he laid it down the moment he saw something truer. He did not cling.

His life asks a quiet question of an ordinary day. What am I holding onto that is smaller than what I could have instead? It invites us to listen before we decide, to spend our standing on what is right, and to stay faithful when no one would blame us for slipping away. Carry one of those into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 33:10). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He was the chief of his tribe.

He had a kingdom within reach, and people who would have followed him to it.

He gave it all away in a single afternoon.

He came to **silence** a teacher.

Mus'ab offered him a seat: sit, listen, and if you dislike it, I will leave.

Sa'd sat down. He never got up the same man.

He stood before his tribe and asked, how do you regard me?

They said: the best of us, the most trustworthy.

Then he said: none of you will speak to me until you believe.

By the end of that one gathering, **every person there had become Muslim.**

With the chief of the Aws, the doors of Madinah opened.

At Badr, the Ansar could have walked away with no blame.

Sa'd stood instead: "If you marched us into the sea, we would march in with you, and not one of us would stay behind."

Struck by an arrow at the Trench, bleeding, he did not pray for himself.

"If there is any fight left with those who hurt Your Prophet, let me live to see it. And if not, let this be my martyrdom."

When he died, the Prophet ﷺ said the **Throne of the Most Merciful shook** at the return of his soul.

They carried his body and felt no weight. The angels were carrying him.

**Seek what lasts, not what dazzles.**

He let go of a throne on earth for what no throne could buy.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (may Allah be pleased with him) was the chief of the Aws in Madinah, the man everyone turned to. He had standing, influence, and a kingdom within reach.

He first came to silence the man teaching Islam in his city. He sat down to listen, and stood up a believer. Days later his whole tribe followed him in. He stayed loyal at Badr when leaving was allowed, and he was struck down defending Madinah at the Trench.

He was Muslim for only about six years. At his death, the Prophet ﷺ said the Throne of the Most Merciful shook.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/saad-ibn-muadh

email.subject: The chief who gave away a kingdom in one afternoon
email.alt_subject: The man for whom the Throne shook
email.preview: He came to silence the truth. He left as its servant, and his whole tribe followed.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (may Allah be pleased with him) was the chief of the Aws, one of the two great tribes of Madinah. He was about thirty-one, and when he walked into a gathering, everyone knew a leader had arrived. He had presence, influence, and a kingdom well within his reach.

When Islam first reached his city, Sa'd did not welcome it. He went, angry and armed, to silence the man teaching it. But that man, Mus'ab ibn Umayr, simply offered him a seat. Sit, listen, and if you do not like what you hear, I will leave.

Sa'd sat down. The anger drained away. He listened, and then he asked the only question that mattered: how do I enter this?

He did not keep it quiet. He stood before his tribe and asked them how they regarded him. The best of us, they said, the most trustworthy. Then he told them that not one of them would speak to him again until they believed in Allah and His Messenger. By the end of that single gathering, every person there had become Muslim.

Years later, at Badr, the Ansar could have turned back without blame, because their pledge had only promised to defend the Prophet ﷺ inside Madinah. Sa'd stood instead. By the One who sent you with the truth, he said, if you marched us into the sea, we would march in with you.

At the Battle of the Trench he was struck by an arrow in the one place his armour did not cover. As he lay bleeding, he prayed not for healing but to see the fight through, and if not, to be taken as a martyr.

He died of that wound, after only about six years as a Muslim. And the Prophet ﷺ said that the Throne of the Most Merciful shook at the return of his soul.

There is a quiet lesson in him. He held more than most of us ever will, and he let it go the moment he saw something truer. He sought what lasts over what merely dazzles.

May Allah be pleased with him, and gather us in the company of the truthful.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (may Allah be pleased with him) was the chief of the Aws, one of the two great tribes of Madinah. He was only about thirty-one, but when he entered a gathering, everyone knew a leader had come. People turned to him to hold their fragile peace together, and he had a kingdom well within his reach.

When a man named Mus'ab began teaching Islam in the city, Sa'd did not like it. He went to the gathering angry and armed, meaning to put an end to it. Mus'ab only offered him a seat. Sit, he said, and listen, and if you do not like what you hear, I will leave.
Sa'd sat down. The anger left him. He listened, and then he asked how he could enter this faith. He prayed two units of prayer, and the man who had come to silence Islam carried it home to his tribe.

He stood before his people and asked how they regarded him. The best of us, they answered, the most trustworthy. Then he told them that not one of them would speak to him again until they believed in Allah and His Messenger. By the end of that single gathering, every person there had become Muslim.

At Badr, the Ansar could have turned back without any blame, for their pledge had only promised to defend the Prophet inside Madinah. Sa'd stood instead, and spoke for them all. By the One who sent you with the truth, he said, if you marched us into the sea, we would follow you in, and not one of us would stay behind.

At the Battle of the Trench he was struck by an arrow in the one spot his armour did not cover, and the wound would not stop bleeding. As the others rushed to save him, he prayed only to see the fight through, and if his time had come, to be taken as a martyr.

He died of that wound, after about six years as a Muslim. The Prophet said the Throne of the Most Merciful shook at the return of his soul. They carried his body and felt almost no weight in it, because, the Prophet said, the angels themselves were carrying him.
storybook.pullquote: If you marched us into the sea, we would march in with you.
storybook.reflection: Sa'd held more than most of us ever will: a name, a tribe, a place at the top. He laid it all down the moment he saw something truer, and never looked back at what he had lost. His life asks one quiet question of an ordinary day. What are we holding onto that is smaller than what we could have instead?
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
